33m Ecobank is a leading financial institution connecting 35 developing and expanding countries across Africa. Our 14,560 employees serve the financial needs of over 32 million (1 million primary banking relationships) businesses and individuals, representing 61% of the continent. As of 31 December 2024, our total assets were $28.0 billion and total eSuity $1.8 billion. Our single payment platform enables real-time payments across Africa to leverage our geographical reach for customers. Our parent company, ETI, is publicly traded on three stock markets: the , the Bourse RȘgionale des Valeurs Mobiliȗres (BRVM) in AbidLan, Côte d'Ivoire, and the Ghana Stock Exchange. Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Section 4 Risk Management Section 3 Corporate Governance Section 2 Group Chairman and Management Section 1 Who we are Papa Madiaw Ndiaye Chairman of the Board "STRONG RESULTS REFLECT THE RESILIENCE OF OUR DIVERSIFIED MODEL AND THE DEDICATION OF ECOBANKERS ACROSS AFRICA." A LETTER FROM THE GROUP CHAIRMAN

Section 5 Business and Financial Review Section 6 Financial Statements Section 7 Corporate Information I am pleased to report that 2024 marked a milestone year - delivering over $2 billion in revenue for the second consecutive year and generating $333 million in profits attributable to ETI shareholders. These strong results reflect the resilience of our diversified model and the dedication of Ecobankers across Africa. But we believe that we can go further. As I present these achievements in my inaugural letter as Chairman, I am guided by two distinct sentiments: first, by a deep sense of responsibility to advance the legacy of this institution; and second, by a profound sense of humility and pride, rooted in my long-standing relationship with Ecobank. My journey with this Bank began in the late 1990s while at the International Finance Corporation (IFC), supporting Ecobank's restructuring and early expansion to align with its ambition of becoming a leading panAfrican institution with a robust institutional shareholder base. Later, as a private equity investor through AFIG Funds, I had the opportunity to invest in and exit from three Ecobank affiliates. Today, as Chairman, these shared experiences continue to reinforce my belief in Ecobank's great potential and the relevance of its mission to be at the heart of Africa's next phase of economic development. Legacy and Vision Ecobank was established with a bold vision: to be a bank for African customers, by an ambitious African team - catalysing growth, integration and prosperity across the continent. Since our founding in 1985 in Lomé, Togo, we have evolved into a continental bank, serving millions of customers across 35 sub-Saharan African countries. We take pride in being the largest bank in terms of geographical reach. In many ways, we have improved the financial lives of numerous African households and businesses, created jobs, contributed our fair share of taxes, and adhered faithfully to increasingly stringent regulatory rules. Ecobankers, past and present, have become some of the most respected bankers across the continent. Many senior African bankers today began or advanced their careers at Ecobank, cementing our reputation as a training hub for Africa's financial leaders. IN RECENT YEARS, ECOBANK HAS EMBARKED ON AN AMBITIOUS STRATEGY TO ACCELERATE GROWTH AND PROFITABILITY. PROFIT ATTRIBUTABLE TO ETI SHAREHOLDERS UP 16% YEARON-YEAR MILLION $333 Dear Shareholders,

Section 4 Risk Management Section 3 Corporate Governance Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Section 1 Who we are Section 2 Group Chairman and Management We have brought numerous innovations to the financial services sector. To name a few: a single unified payments gateway, an award-winning multi-country, multi-currency mobile app, and real-time cross-border payments. And we are not finished yet. We are now doubling down on digital and data-driven solutions that connect customers, markets and capital across borders with greater speed and ease. We are continuing to power and improve seamless connectivity across Africa, as this is fundamental to our ongoing and future operating model. This is just one of the many ways in which we are empowering our Corporate and Investment Banking and Commercial Banking clients to grow their businesses by grasping the immense opportunities created by the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA)'s creation of a single African market. These accomplishments are laudable, and we owe our thanks to the dedicated men and women of Ecobank, both past and present, who have made this possible. However, as we cast our eyes into the future and reimagine all possibilities - rising competition from banks, fintechs and non-bank financial institutions, as well as factors such as geopolitics, regulation, capital markets, evolving customer behaviours, artificial intelligence and talent acquisition - we cannot afford complacency. This means it is imperative that we embrace our ongoing 'Transformation' agenda with renewed urgency to achieve sustainable growth. Acknowledging the Legacy of My Predecessor Before moving forward, I want to acknowledge the immense contributions of Alain Nkontchou, my predecessor as Chairman. Alain's leadership steered the Bank through a crucial transition period. Under his guidance, Ecobank returned to profitability, entrenched governance discipline, and made the prospect of dividend payments after years of restructuring very real. I thank him sincerely for his service to Ecobank. Positive 2024 Financial Performance $4.2* $262 $286 $288 $333 Attributable profit impacted by a $164m goodwill charge ($15m related to Oceanic Bank acSuired in 2011 and $4m related to SOFIPE, our microfinance entity acSuired in 2014), $61m in net monetary losses (due to hyperinƔation in <imababwe and South Sudan), $44m in legal and restructuring costs. EPS Attributable profit to EI shareholders ROE 0.3% 0.01 1.17 1.17 1.06 19.0% 21.1% 24.9% 32.7% 1.36 Group Profit After Tax Attributable to ETI Shareholders (in millions of US dollars), ROTE (%), and EPS (US$ cents ) 2024 was a positive year. Your company generated $333 million in profits attributable to ETI (the Holdco) shareholders, a 16% increase year-on-year, based on net revenue of $2.1 billion. We also raised your earnings per share by 16%, bringing it to $0.014 (or 1.355 $ cents), and increased the tangible book value per share (TBVPS) by 4% to $0.042 (or 4.20 $ cents). Additionally, we achieved a record return on tangible shareholders' equity (ROTE) of 32.7%. Our total assets on the balance sheet grew by 3% to $28.0 billion, including customer deposits of $20.4 billion and loans of $10.5 billion. The balance sheet remains liquid, with a loan-to-deposit ratio of 51.4%, down from 55.4% the previous year. Despite the negative impact of the reserves from translating subsidiaries' balance sheets into US dollars, our Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio improved by 100 basis points to 11.4% in 2024. Our underlying cost-toincome ratio also improved to a record 53.0%, reflecting operational discipline and strategic platform leverage. The Chairman of the Board of Ecobank Group and the CESA Regional Executive/MD of Ecobank Penya engaged with staff at the Head Office branch in Nairobi to promote excellence and recognise their efforts in September 2024. 22% YEAR-ON-YEAR DIVIDEND INCREASE FROM SUBSIDIARIES 2024 dividends received of $217m vs $178m in 2023

Section 5 Business and Financial Review Section 6 Financial Statements Section 7 Corporate Information These results underscore the ongoing strength of our geographically diversified business model, the resilience of our underlying businesses, and the dedication of Ecobankers to serve our customers and deliver returns to our shareholders. However, these metrics tell only part of the story. Our delivery culture is evolving. Previously underperforming affiliates across regions from Uganda to Equatorial Guinea are now making significant contributions. Our capital markets strategy was validated by successfully issuing a $400 million Eurobond and launching West Africa's first gender bond - clear signals of investor trust and confidence. Reinforcing the Dividend Discipline We understand that dividends are important to all our shareholders. Of the 641,000 shareholders of ETI, about 600,000 own fewer than 10,000 units and rely on dividend payments. We acknowledge that not paying dividends again is disappointing and that explaining our reasoning is crucial. The dividend payment process has two aspects: dividends from subsidiaries and those paid to shareholders. On a positive note, 22 subsidiaries paid record dividends totalling $217 million this year, reflecting a 22% increase, and we anticipate continued growth in these payouts. However, given our focus on strengthening the balance sheet to pave the way for accelerated growth, we faced the difficult decision of either servicing the debt and complying with existing debt covenants or paying dividends. Acting in the best interest of the company, we made the difficult decision not to pay dividends this year. (1) The cash dividends received annually from dividend-paying subsidiaries are based on their attributable profits from the previous year to ETI. For instance, the $217 million dividends received in 2024 correspond to the attributable profits for ETI from 2023. (2) Others include our International business (i.e., EBI SA Groupe Ecobank, our Paris-based subsidiary), EDC, our Investment Banking and Wealth and Asset Management businesses, and eProcess, our operations and technology business. * Nigeria has not paid a dividend to ETI within the 2020-2024 period. # dividend-paying subsidiaries $91 $93 $122 $178 $217 UEMOA NIGERIA AWA CESA OTHERS2 Dividends Paid by Subsidiaries1 to ETI (in millions of US dollars) Dividend paid to ETI shareholders Attributable profit to EI Attributable profit impacted by a $164m goodwill charge ($15m related to Oceanic Bank acSuired in 2011 and $4m related toSOFIPE, our microfinance entity acSuired in 2014), $61m in net monetary losses (due to hyperinƔation in <imababwe and South Sudan), $44m in legal and restructuring costs. $40 $28 $4.2* $262 $286 $288 $333 Group Profit After Tax Attributable Profit to ETI Shareholders and Dividends Paid by Subsidiaries to ETI (in millions of US dollars) The Discounted Value of ETI Stock: A Candid Assessment Ecobank's market value at year-end stood at $447 million, down 21% year-on-year, partly due to the devaluation of the Nigerian NGN, while the accounting book value of your equity is $1.08 billion. The price per share was $0.02, while the tangible book value per share was $0.04, representing a market price-to-book value of 0.43x. The deeply discounted value of our stock relative to our book value is a stark and humbling reality - one that cannot be attributed to a single cause. Several internal and external factors influence share prices. The valuation may reflect issues such as asset quality, capital and profitability concerns in Nigeria, the complexity of our Group structure, corporate governance challenges, Group-wide capital adequacy, and the lack of regular dividend payouts. Additionally, macroeconomic and regulatory factors affecting the banking sector, competitive dynamics, and geopolitical risks also play a role. (1) Tangible book value per share (TBVPS) is Tangible Shareholders' Equity (TSE) at period-end divided by ordinary shares outstanding at period-end. TSE is ETI Shareholders' Equity less intangibles. 5.47 5.70 5.30 4.04 4.20 1.81 2.29 2.30 2.05 1.51 TBVPS(1) Share price ETI's Share Price and Tangible Book Value Per Share1 (in US dollar cents)

16% YEAR-ON-YEAR GROWTH IN EPS Section 4 Risk Management Section 3 Corporate Governance Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Section 1 Who we are Section 2 Group Chairman and Management ETI NGN Share price NGX All-Share Index NGX Banking Index 430.77 383.45 303.92 Source: Bloomberg Performance of ETI's Share Price (NGN) vs. NG¦ All-Share Index and NG¦ Banking Index (indexed to closing prices as of 31 December 2019) Although external factors are beyond our control, we can still influence outcomes through our internal actions and execution. Our Growth, Transformation and Returns (GTR) strategy focuses on operational excellence to enhance customer satisfaction and drive revenue growth. We continue to empower and reward our employees while reinforcing strong execution and maintaining discipline. Our goal is to deliver superior returns to shareholders. By effectively implementing these strategies, we can positively impact our market valuation. 2020 ROTE impacted by a $164m goodwill charge, $61m in net monetary losses, and $44m in legal and restructuring costs. Equity Attributable to ETI Shareholders ROTE $1.50 $1.53 $1.39 $1.05 $1.08 32.7% 24.9% 21.1% 19.0% 0.3% Shareholders' Equity Attributable to ETI (in US$ billions) and ROTE (%) Turning Around Underperforming Affiliates Some of our affiliates - such as Nigeria - have experienced significant macroeconomic volatility along with specific sector challenges. The resulting performances in such countries have also negatively affected market sentiment. The Group CEO's letter provides more detail on this, and I want to reaffirm that the Board and management collaborate closely to enhance execution, governance and risk management, across all our underperforming affiliates and throughout the Group. While we remain committed to these markets, we will balance this against our confidence in their long-term value contribution to the Group. Every single underperforming affiliate, starting with Nigeria, will be given the opportunity and the support to rapidly find the path to profitability and growth. Over the next year, we look forward to seeing Nigeria begin to fulfil its promise not only as a solid revenue driver, but also as a hub for innovation and talent to be a leader in areas such as payments, digital onboarding and for financing small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). The Case for a Modern Pan-African Strategy Ecobank's pan-African vision - and indeed our support for the AfCFTA - is vital but must also create real value. Strong value creation could reduce the company's cost of equity and expand its community programmes, including our corporate social responsibility initiatives and the impactful work of the Ecobank Foundation. Therefore, we must continually ask ourselves: Is being pan-African delivering the returns our shareholders expect? In 2024, the results across the geographies were mixed. While Zimbabwe and Kenya did not meet expectations, Ghana, Guinea and Burkina Faso exceeded them. This demonstrates the power of diversification, which must ultimately lead to increased shareholder value. We remain committed to unlocking the full potential of our network - integrating platforms, harmonising standards, and enabling the smooth flow of capital, goods and data across borders. As African governments have stepped up their commitment to increasing intra-African trade, Ecobank is well-positioned to play a key role in this acceleration.

Section 5 Business and Financial Review Section 6 Financial Statements Section 7 Corporate Information Corporate Governance and Stewardship Sound governance underpins sustainable value creation. As Chairman, I view governance not as a compliance exercise but as a strategic enabler of performance, trust and long-term resilience. The Board remains firmly committed to robust oversight, constructive challenge, and an unwavering focus on shareholder interests. As the Commonsense Principles of Corporate Governance remind us, effective boards must be diverse, independent in thought, and aligned with the company's long-term values. These are principles we take seriously at Ecobank and continuously strive to embed them in our governance culture. As we look ahead, shareholders can expect the following from my tenure as Chairman: υ Sharper strategic oversight: The Board will continue to ensure the Group's strategic priorities are clearly defined, well executed, and rigorously monitored - particularly in capital allocation, risk management, digital transformation, and business and regional performance. υ More transparent executive accountability: Leadership must deliver results and do so in a way that reflects our values. We are strengthening how we assess performance - linking strategy, culture and results more tightly. υ Executive compensation aligned with performance: Pay must reflect delivery. The Board's Remuneration Committee reviews incentive structures to ensure that they reward sustainable value creation, not short-term metrics. This includes further integrating Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) progress, cost discipline, and return on equity (ROE) into evaluating executive performance. υ Enhanced shareholder engagement: We are deepening dialogue with the investment community to ensure that concerns are heard, and that progress is communicated transparently. This includes more regular updates, open forums, and a refreshed approach to investor relations. υ Succession planning and board renewal: Long-term success requires a future-ready leadership pipeline. We are committed to strengthening executive and board succession planning and ensuring that we attract and retain talent that reflects the complexity and opportunity of our pan-African mandate. Good governance is about creating the conditions for sustainable success. It is also about accountability. We know trust is not declared - it is earned through performance, transparency and alignment with our stakeholders. That is the ethos that we are bringing to the next chapter of Ecobank's governance journey. With the capable group of board members I have with me, I am confident about our collective ability to deliver on the above. Looking Ahead: A New Chapter As Ecobank turns 40 years old, we stand at a defining moment in our journey. Over the past five years, Ecobank has undergone a courageous but steady transformation. We continue to make meaningful progress in tackling legacy challenges around credit quality and cost efficiency. Over recent years, the Group has returned to profitability, strengthened its capital base, and leaned fully into digitisation. Investments in mobile banking, digital payments, technology platforms, and digital onboarding were not just technological shifts - they became strategic tools to expand reach and reposition Ecobank as the go-to pan-African platform for individuals, SMEs, governments and corporates. Now, with Jeremy Awori as CEO and a new phase of execution ahead, we are entering a new era. The GTR strategy is improving the company's performance metrics and overall condition. Above all, it is meeting consumers and SMEs evolving needs, leading in trade finance and payments, sharpening focus, and reasserting our role as a trusted financial bridge across Africa in the context of the implementation of the AfCFTA. The early signs are encouraging - return on equity is rising, cost-to-income is falling, and our digital investments are giving us a real edge. If the past decade may have been about laying foundations and fixing the engine, the next one is about acceleration - driven by clarity, confidence and purpose. As we say in Africa: "No matter how long the night, the day is sure to come." The foundations have been laid. The dawn of a stronger, more agile Ecobank has arrived. We are poised to lead. The future will reward those who are connected, adaptive and bold. This is the Ecobank that we are building. A Shared Responsibility To our shareholders and investors: thank you for your patience and belief. To our clients: thank you for trusting us to serve you. To our employees: thank you for delivering every day. To our regulators and partners: thank you for your support. As we enter our fifth decade, our focus is not on nostalgia, but on building and sustaining momentum. Together, let us build the Ecobank that Africa needs, trusts and our shareholders deserve. Sincerely, Papa Madiaw Ndiaye Chairman of the Board

Section 1 Who we are Section 4 Risk Management Section 2 Group Chairman and Management Section 3 Corporate Governance Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Section 4 Risk Management Section 3 Corporate Governance Section 2 Group Chairman and Management Jeremy Awori Group %hief Executive Officer "OUR GTR STRATEGY IS CLEAR: GROW OUR BUSINESS, TRANSFORM HOW WE SERVE OUR CUSTOMERS, AND INCREASE RETURNS THROUGH CONSISTENT EXECUTION AND EFFICIENT CAPITAL ALLOCATION. WE ARE NOT HERE FOR SHORT-TERM WINS. WE ARE HERE TO SHAPE THE FINANCIAL FUTURE OF AFRICA." A LETTER FROM THE GROUP CEO

Section 5 Business and Financial Review Section 6 Financial Statements Section 7 Corporate Information I am pleased to report that we are making progress in many areas thanks to the remarkable eagerness of all Ecobankers to learn and embrace the changes that come with this transformation. Our transformation journey builds on the foundations laid to create a strong pan-African franchise over the past 40 years. We are continuing this journey guided by the principles of our Growth, Transformation and Returns (GTR) strategy. In 2024, we focused on strengthening our infrastructure, redefining our priorities, and aligning every part of our organisation around our strategic objectives. While we are already seeing positive results, our ultimate objective is to accelerate our progress and build a business model that will endure for decades. A Pan-African Institution with Purpose We are a truly diversified pan-African financial services group, impacting lives across 35 African countries - as well as globally, through our diaspora banking network. From enabling trade and facilitating payments to financing dreams and driving financial inclusion, we support growth, opportunity and progress wherever our customers are. In 2024, we reaffirmed our position as Africa's leading bank - resilient, purpose-driven and forward-looking. We were proud to be named Africa's Best Bank by Global Finance, recognising our leadership in the African banking sector. Laying the Foundation for Sustainable Growth To us, growth is not just about bigger numbers - it is about growing in ways that matter. It means reaching more people, staying relevant, and making a genuine difference in our customers' lives. That is the kind of growth we value. We will grow by helping more customers open primary current accounts, save and invest with us, transfer money faster and more affordably both locally and across borders, and last but not least, plan for their future using products like mortgages and wealth solutions. In 2024, we began reorienting the Bank to achieve these goals, while always remembering that long-term revenue growth remains the key driver of returns, value and sustainably high returns on equity. Driving Revenue, Returns and Regional Performance In 2024, Group-wide net revenues rose 1%, or 18% at constant currency, to $2.1 billion, with a record return on tangible equity (ROTE) of 32.7%. In Corporate and Investment Banking (CIB), revenues rose by 16% in constant currency to $1.1 billion, fuelled by our leadership position in Francophone West Africa (UEMOA) and Anglophone West Africa (AWA), and strong performance in core products such as cash management, payments and trade. Structured trade finance disbursements reached $450 million, while cash management brought in $500 million in deposits. Separately, our successful Eurobond repurchase for Gabon, totalling $420 million, demonstrated African capital markets expertise. In Consumer and Commercial Banking, revenues increased by 15%, and by 12% at constant currency, reaching $504 million and $566 million, respectively. Active agent ratios rose from 19% to 29%. Card revenue increased by 11% year-on-year, and Consumer Banking improved product holdings per customer from 1.2 to 1.5. These numbers point to the opportunity that still lies ahead - particularly through partnerships and primary relationship growth. While we serve millions of customers, our market share across key segments remains modest - approximately 10% in Corporate, 6.5% in Commercial and 3.5% in Consumer. This under-penetration highlights the significant headroom available to us. In 2024, we brought our Consumer and Commercial Banking businesses under WHEN I TOOK OVER ECOBANK'S LEADERSHIP IN 2023, WE ESTABLISHED AN AMBITIOUS GOAL TO TRANSFORM HOW WE SERVE AND CONNECT WITH OUR CUSTOMERS ACROSS AFRICA. Dear Shareholders, 32.7% RECORD ROTE (2023:24.9%)

Section 4 Risk Management Section 3 Corporate Governance Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Ecobank Group Annual Report 2024 Section 1 Who we are Section 2 Group Chairman and Management one leadership team - creating a unified, leaner and more focused Consumer and Commercial Banking (CCB) structure. While they remain strong, yet distinct businesses, this move helps us work more closely across both areas, through a clear segmentation strategy, making it easier to deliver for our customers and drive shared success. Bringing the two businesses together helps us focus better on each customer group, simplifies our products, and builds stronger relationships, particularly in markets where our brand strength is pronounced, such as UEMOA and AWA. On a regional basis, revenues grew when adjusted for constant currency. In the UEMOA region, where we maintain a market-leading position, revenues increased by 6% to $705 million, driven by higher in

chairman_statement.text:
Chairman of the Board "STRONG RESULTS REFLECT THE RESILIENCE OF OUR DIVERSIFIED MODEL AND THE DEDICATION OF ECOBANKERS ACROSS AFRICA." A LETTER FROM THE GROUP CHAIRMAN

Section 5 Business and Financial Review Section 6 Financial Statements Section 7 Corporate Information I am pleased to report that 2024 marked a milestone year - delivering over $2 billion in revenue for the second consecutive year and generating $333 million in profits attributable to ETI shareholders. These strong results reflect the resilience of our diversified model and the dedication of Ecobankers across Africa. But we believe that we can go further. As I present these achievements in my inaugural letter as Chairman, I am guided by two distinct sentiments: first, by a deep sense of responsibility to advance the legacy of this institution; and second, by a profound sense of humility and pride, rooted in my long-standing relationship with Ecobank. My journey with this Bank began in the late 1990s while at the International Finance Corporation (IFC), supporting Ecobank's restructuring and early expansion to align with its ambition of becoming a leading panAfrican institution with a robust institutional shareholder base. Later, as a private equity investor through AFIG Funds, I had the opportunity to invest in and exit from three Ecobank affiliates. Today, as Chairman, these shared experiences continue to reinforce my belief in Ecobank's great potential and the relevance of its mission to be at the heart of Africa's next phase of economic development. Legacy and Vision Ecobank was established with a bold vision: to be a bank for African customers, by an ambitious African team - catalysing growth, integration and prosperity across the continent. Since our founding in 1985 in Lomé, Togo, we have evolved into a continental bank, serving millions of customers across 35 sub-Saharan African countries. We take pride in being the largest bank in terms of geographical reach. In many ways, we have improved the financial lives of numerous African households and businesses, created jobs, contributed our fair share of taxes, and adhered faithfully to increasingly stringent regulatory rules. Ecobankers, past and present, have become some of the most respected bankers across the continent. Many senior African bankers today began or advanced their careers at Ecobank, cementing our reputation as a training hub for Africa's financial leaders. IN RECENT YEARS, ECOBANK HAS EMBARKED ON AN AMBITIOUS STRATEGY TO ACCELERATE GROWTH AND PROFITABILITY. PROFIT ATTRIBUTABLE TO ETI SHAREHOLDERS UP 16% YEARON-YEAR MILLION $333 Dear Shareholders,

Section 5 Business and Financial Review Section 6 Financial Statements Section 7 Corporate Information Number of meetings of the Board Committees during 2024: Governance, Nomination, Remuneration and Ethics Committee Audit, Internal Control and Compliance Committee Finance, Risk and Credit Committee Information Technology, Social and Reputation Committee Board Committees As at 31 December 2024, the Board operated through four (4) standing Committees, each mandated to support effective governance through focused oversight and specialised expertise: 1. Governance, Nomination, Remuneration and Ethics Committee; 2. Audit, Internal Control and Compliance Committee; 3. Finance, Risk and Credit Committee; and 4. Information Technology, Social and Reputation Committee. These Committees are established in line with global best practice and the specific regulatory requirements of the jurisdictions in which ETI operates. Their charters clearly define their responsibilities, authority and reporting obligations. Membership excludes Executive Directors and consists primarily of Independent Non-Executive Directors and nominees of major institutional shareholders. Each Committee is chaired by an Independent Director, reinforcing impartiality, objectivity and robust challenge. Also, for the purposes of revitalising the Board Committees, the tenure of members is restricted to a maximum of two (2), three (3)-year terms, which may be extended, if it is considered appropriate. In 2024, in application of this rule, the composition of board committees was restructured to take account of the competencies of the directors and compliance with applicable rules. Board Committees' membership Governance, Nomination, Remuneration and Ethics Committee The Governance, Nomination, Remuneration and Ethics Committee met seven (7) times in 2024 to deliberate on matters within its mandate. Its members bring extensive experience in corporate governance across multiple jurisdictions, ensuring high-quality oversight of the Group's leadership, ethics and people strategy. Composition and attendance Name Role Meetings eligible to attend Meetings attended Prof. Enase Okonedo Chairperson Mr. Hervé Assah1 Member Mr. Simon Dornoo2 Member Mr. Brian Kennedy1 Member Mr. Deepak Malik Member Dr. Catherine Ngahu Member Mr. Mfundo Nkuhlu1 Member Dr. Aasim Qureshi2 Member 1. Retired in June 2024 2. Joined the Board Committee in June 2024 The Group General Counsel and Company Secretary or his designate serves as Secretary to the Committee. Responsibilities of the Governance, Nomination, Remuneration and Ethics Committee: • Formulates, reviews and ensures implementation of policies applicable to all units of the Group, as well as good governance throughout the Group. • Manages the relationship between the Company and its shareholders and subsidiaries, including relationships with the Boards of subsidiaries.

outlook.text:
Outlook for 2025 In 2025, Ecobank will continue to strengthen its risk management ecosystem through strategic investments in predictive analytics, early warning systems and dynamic risk modelling. As the operating environment remains shaped by persistent inflation, currency volatility and shifting regulatory expectations, we will deepen scenario-based planning and stress testing to ensure robust capital and liquidity management. Seamless connectivity across the Group's digital platforms will remain central to enhancing real-time risk intelligence and reporting. We will expand our cybersecurity capabilities in line with emerging threats, further automate operational risk controls, and reinforce compliance infrastructure across high-risk jurisdictions. Building on our diversified footprint and integrated control framework, Ecobank is well-positioned to manage emerging risks while supporting sustainable business growth. Our focus in the year ahead will be on embedding agility into risk governance, ensuring we respond proactively to change while preserving value for all stakeholders.

Section 5 Business and Financial Review Section 6
